parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may read, modify, or delete database data or escalate to further SQL execution depending on the DB driver and permissions.
parsed_findings.proof: With `sort=name` (allowed) and a malicious `dir` value, attacker-controlled SQL fragments execute as part of the `ORDER BY` expression.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request with `sort=name&dir=asc,(SELECT 1)` or another DB-specific `ORDER BY` payload and observe query behavior or errors indicating injection.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `dir` query parameter is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause without allowlisting, enabling SQL injection while `sort` is whitelisted.

parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can coerce the server to reach internal hosts or cloud metadata and exfiltrate responses via the reflected body.
parsed_findings.proof: req.query.url is assigned to u and passed to axios.get(u) while line 3 begins a line comment after if (, so the apparent host check cannot enforce a safe target.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue GET /fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ (or any attacker-controlled listener) and observe the server fetch and return that response body.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler forwards a user-controlled URL to axios.get without a working allowlist, causing server-side request forgery.

parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may cause unauthorized invoice note changes on behalf of a logged-in user.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker-controlled page can trigger a PATCH to this route with the victim’s cookies, mutating inv.note without user intent.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Log in, open a malicious page that issues fetch PATCH /api/invoices/<id> with credentials included, and observe the invoice updates.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: State-changing PATCH uses session-style current_user without any visible CSRF token or double-submit defense, enabling classic cookie-session CSRF if browsers send session cookies on cross-site requests.

parsed_findings.impact: Unexpected null note values may break downstream validation, display, or integrity expectations.
parsed_findings.proof: Body {"note":null} sets inv.note to None rather than preserving the prior note.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH with {"note":null} and verify the stored note becomes null.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Using request.json.get('note', inv.note) allows JSON null to overwrite the note because the key is present and the default is skipped.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Single-step: malformed file query bypasses weak sanitizer then readFile serves the escaped path.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary file read for paths the process can access, breaking intended confinement to /srv/data.
parsed_findings.proof: For file=../../etc/passwd, f becomes ../etc/passwd and path.join('/srv/data', f) resolves outside /srv/data (e.g. /srv/etc/passwd).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd (or similar depth) and observe non-404 read of a path outside /srv/data.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Path traversal because only the first '../' is stripped so remaining '..' segments still escape the intended base via path.join normalization.

parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution in the app server process.
parsed_findings.proof: `pickle.loads` can instantiate arbitrary classes and run gadget chains, so a crafted `state` cookie yields code execution.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set `Cookie: state=<base64(pickle payload)>` and request `GET /resume` so the server unpickles it.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `state` cookie is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, deserializing attacker-controlled data.

parsed_findings.impact: Unauthorized user profile updates including cross-account modification.
parsed_findings.proof: `if (req.user.id req.user.isSupport)` is invalid JavaScript and does not express a coherent authorization rule tying `req.user` to `req.params.id`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Attempt to parse/run the route as written; observe syntax failure or, if “fixed” incorrectly, PATCH `/api/users/:id` as a non-owner.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The guard condition is malformed (missing boolean/relational operator), so intended owner-or-support checks likely never compile or never enforce correctly.

parsed_findings.impact: Privilege escalation or integrity compromise via writable model attributes.
parsed_findings.proof: All enumerable properties from `req.body` are copied into `user` before `db.users.save(user)` without an allowlist.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH `/api/users/:id` with JSON containing sensitive/privileged keys (e.g., role/admin flags) present in the user model.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `Object.assign(user, req.body)` performs unsafe mass assignment of attacker-controlled fields onto the persisted user object.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Broken/missing authz at L2 enables reaching L5–L7 against arbitrary ids.
parsed_findings.impact: Cross-account data modification.
parsed_findings.proof: Persistence targets `findById(req.params.id)` while the only shown gate does not compare `req.user.id` to `req.params.id`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: As an authenticated low-privilege user, PATCH a different `:id` and observe whether the foreign user row updates.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User lookup uses `req.params.id` without a correct same-user authorization tie-in in the shown guard, enabling IDOR-style updates if access control is bypassed or misimplemented.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Standalone stored XSS from Markdown HTML passthrough without needing a second bug.
parsed_findings.impact: Session theft, account takeover actions, or defacement for any user viewing injected comments.
parsed_findings.proof: Default Markdown rendering outputs attacker-supplied tags/attributes from the body field into rendered HTML saved as html.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /comments with a body containing an HTML payload such as , then view any page that echoes stored comments as HTML.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted comment text is passed through Markdown into HTML persisted for later rendering, which typically preserves raw HTML and yields stored XSS.

parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can arbitrarily inflate their own balance while debiting another wallet id.
parsed_findings.proof: With src=10 and amt=-100, src<amt is false, sender becomes src-amt=110, and recipient balance is reduced by 100 via db.get_balance(to)+amt.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer with JSON to set to a victim wallet id and amount set to a large negative integer while sender has a small positive balance.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The insufficient-funds guard compares src to amt without rejecting non-positive amounts, so negative amt bypasses the check and inverts the balance updates.

parsed_findings.impact: Double-spend or negative balance inconsistent with intended ledger integrity.
parsed_findings.proof: Two parallel transfers can both observe the same src before either debit lands, passing src<amt individually while combined debits exceed src.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two simultaneous POST /transfer requests each with amount equal to the full available balance.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Read-check-write on balances is not wrapped in a single atomic transaction or row lock, enabling concurrent requests to overdraw the same account.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Standalone auth bypass; pair with privileged routes for full account takeover.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can forge tokens to impersonate users or elevate privileges despite failed cryptographic verification.
parsed_findings.proof: jwt.decode only parses base64url segments and never checks RS256 signatures, so a forged token still yields a decoded object after verify throws.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: In NODE_ENV=production, send a Bearer JWT with an invalid signature or wrong key and observe verifyToken still returning the decoded claims.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: When jwt.verify fails in production, the handler returns jwt.decode(t), which accepts arbitrary payloads without signature verification.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: None; this is a direct authenticated IDOR read without chaining.
parsed_findings.impact: Confidential user data (email) can be enumerated across accounts, aiding phishing and account takeover targeting.
parsed_findings.proof: After login_required passes, uid from the query drives a primary-key lookup that returns another user’s row with no ownership or role check shown.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticate, then GET /u?id=<victim_id> and observe the JSON includes that user’s id and email.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The route lets any logged-in caller supply an arbitrary user id with no binding to the session user, so object-level authorization is missing before returning id and email.

parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may be able to forge or brute-force a valid signature faster than the intended bit-security through timing analysis of verification.
parsed_findings.proof: `got == want` branches on each byte comparison in a way that can leak how many prefix bytes match unless `hmac.compare_digest` is used.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Repeatedly submit requests whose `X-Sig` header differs from the legitimate tag by one byte at a time and statistically compare server response times across positions.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The HMAC tag is verified with ordinary string equality instead of a constant-time comparison, creating a timing side channel on the signature bytes.

parsed_findings.impact: This harms availability of the webhook endpoint and may leak diagnostic details about failures depending on deployment configuration.
parsed_findings.proof: Invalid UTF-8 or JSON syntax in `body` after a passing MAC causes `json.loads` to throw rather than return a controlled error.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: After crafting a body whose HMAC verifies but JSON is invalid, POST it and observe an unhandled exception or generic server error.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `json.loads(body)` is not wrapped in error handling, so malformed JSON can raise and terminate the request handler unexpectedly.

parsed_findings.impact: Trusted-domain links can be weaponized for phishing or OAuth/OIDC code or token theft via malicious redirects.
parsed_findings.proof: A request to `/go?next=//evil.com` yields `redirect('//evil.com')`, producing a `Location` header browsers resolve to another host.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue `GET /go?next=//evil.com` and confirm the response redirects the browser away from the application origin.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `startswith('/')` guard still allows protocol-relative URLs like `//evil.com`, which bypass same-origin intent and redirect off-site.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Often paired with follow-on access to internal HTTP services or filesystem paths reachable from the parser process.
parsed_findings.impact: Potential local file disclosure, SSRF via URL-loading entities, or sensitive data exfiltration depending on parser and platform limits.
parsed_findings.proof: resolve_entities=True with no_network=False lets libxml2 resolve user-supplied entities, commonly enabling file/http entity references during fromstring(request.data).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST to /xml/import with a DTD declaring an external SYSTEM entity pointing to a collaborator URL or file path and reference that entity in the document.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: XXE risk: XMLParser resolves entities with no_network=False, so attacker XML can trigger external/public entity resolution.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Primarily degrades availability; may be chained with timeouts or worker pool exhaustion for broader outage.
parsed_findings.impact: CPU and memory exhaustion leading to denial of service for the request-handling worker or process.
parsed_findings.proof: resolve_entities=True processes DTD-defined entities, allowing crafted XML to expand to huge in-memory trees or deep recursion during expansion.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST XML containing a recursive or highly nested INTERNAL/PE entity definition that expands exponentially when parsed.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: DoS risk: entity resolution enables recursive/parameter entity expansion attacks (e.g., billion laughs) against the parser.

parsed_findings.impact: Authentication bypass or account takeover by manipulating query semantics rather than knowing the correct password.
parsed_findings.proof: Sending JSON like `{"email":{"$ne":null},"password":{"$ne":null}}` can match arbitrary users instead of exact credentials.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/login` with a JSON body containing `$ne`/`$gt` operators in `email` or `password` fields and observe unexpected authentication success.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.body` fields are spread into a MongoDB-style `findOne` query object, enabling NoSQL operator injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: SSRF to instance metadata retrieves temporary cloud credentials for lateral movement.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can scan or abuse internal services, hit cloud metadata APIs, or pivot on the server's network trust.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 3 assigns url from JSON and line 6 calls requests.get(url) while line 4 only rejects non-http schemes, not private IPs, link-local addresses, or redirect targets.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /avatar/fetch with body {"url":"http://127.0.0.1:8080/"} (or http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ in a cloud VM) and observe the server perform the outbound GET.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled URL is fetched server-side after only a trivial http prefix check, enabling SSRF to internal hosts and metadata endpoints.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pair many parallel /avatar/fetch calls with large fast responses to amplify denial of service.
parsed_findings.impact: Elevated memory use can degrade or crash the application worker.
parsed_findings.proof: requests.get(url, timeout=2).content buffers the entire body regardless of Content-Length or stream size.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Point url at a server that returns a very large body quickly within the 2s window and repeat concurrent requests.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Full HTTP response is read into memory via .content with no explicit size limit, enabling large-response memory pressure.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Single-step primitive: unbounded stack write from gets alone is typically sufficient for exploitation.
parsed_findings.impact: Crash or attacker-controlled execution via overwritten return address or stack metadata.
parsed_findings.proof: Any input longer than 64 bytes writes past buf’s end, corrupting adjacent stack slots including the return address.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Build and run vuln.c, then provide more than 64 bytes on stdin (e.g., a long piped line) to observe overflow behavior.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: gets reads unlimited stdin into buf[64] with no bounds check, enabling a stack buffer overflow.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Often chained with backup/export, rooting, or malware with storage access rather than remote exploitation alone.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker with device compromise, backup access, or physical extraction can steal session tokens and impersonate the user.
parsed_findings.proof: putString stores the bearer token in cleartext XML under the app’s prefs file reachable to rooted users, backups, or forensic extraction.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: On a test device, pull `/data/data/<pkg>/shared_prefs/` XML or enable backup and observe the stored access_token value in plaintext.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The access token is written to SharedPreferences without encryption or hardware-backed protection despite MODE_PRIVATE sandboxing.

parsed_findings.impact: Anyone with code or binary access can derive the key and decrypt or forge ciphertext at will.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 2 assigns KEY = b'0123456789abcdef', which is extractable from the binary or repo and reused for every encryption.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read crypto_util.py and copy the KEY constant to decrypt any ciphertext produced by enc().
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES key is hard-coded in source as a static 16-byte literal.

parsed_findings.impact: Adversaries who can modify ciphertext may tamper with plaintext or attack decrypting endpoints depending on usage.
parsed_findings.proof: AES.new(..., MODE_CBC, iv) followed by encrypt omits any MAC or AEAD tag in the returned iv||ciphertext.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Produce enc(data), flip ciphertext bits, and if a decrypt path exists it may accept altered plaintext or leak via padding/oracle behavior.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC encryption returns ciphertext with no integrity or authenticity protection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Not chained; defect is self-contained in this function.
parsed_findings.impact: Undefined behavior and likely crash from writing through a null pointer.
parsed_findings.proof: For `out == NULL` and `out_sz > 0`, `out` is falsy and `out_sz == 0` is false, so the function does not return and executes `memcpy(out, in, n)` with a null destination when `n > 0`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Invoke `copy_name("x", NULL, 2)` (or any nonempty `in` with `out == NULL` and `out_sz` large enough that `n > 0`).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The condition `if (out || out_sz == 0)` is inverted so a NULL `out` with positive `out_sz` bypasses the guard and reaches `memcpy`.
